courses.title: CRUD
courses.description: A premium teacher-led React CRUD course with real business scenarios, quizzes, and hands-on code exams.

steps.title: Course introduction
steps.description: Meet the course and learn what you will build.
steps.contentHtml: NA
detailedInstructions: Instructor: Welcome learners — explain that this workshop covers CRUD fundamentals in React with real examples and hands-on exercises.
Explain the course flow: short lesson, code exam, then a quiz to confirm understanding. Encourage questions and live experimentation.
Class tip: emphasize immutability early — it's the key to predictable UI updates in React.

steps.title: Real-world scenario
steps.description: See CRUD in action across POS, logistics, and admin systems.
steps.contentHtml:
CRUD in the real world

CRUD workflows appear in many business applications. Examples include:

  • Point-of-sale systems where clerks add customers, products, and transactions.
  • Logistics dashboards that track orders, shipments, and inventory status.
  • Admin portals used to manage users, settings, and support tickets.

These apps need reliable create, read, update, and delete behavior to keep data consistent and easy to manage.

steps.title: Why React for CRUD
steps.description: Learn about React's popularity and the roles that use it.
steps.contentHtml:
Why React?

React is a leading frontend library that maps well to data-driven UIs. It helps structure apps into components and manage dynamic state simply.

  • Component-based architecture for reuse and composition.
  • Lightweight rendering model for fast UI updates.
  • Fits well for CRUD workflows and interactive dashboards.
detailedInstructions: Instructor notes: Explain component boundaries and when to lift state up vs. using context. Use the diagram to show how state, UI and actions interact.
Demo: Show a tiny function component that renders a list and updates it with setState — explain re-rendering behavior.

steps.title: React fundamentals quiz
steps.description: Confirm your understanding of React basics before coding.

quizQuestions.prompt: Which hook is used to store simple state in a function component?
quizQuestions.explanation: useState is the standard hook for local component state such as form values and lists.

quizQuestions.prompt: What happens when React state changes?

options.text: The component re-renders with updated UI

quizQuestions.explanation: React updates the component output and applies the changed UI to the DOM.

steps.title: Code exam: render a list
steps.description: Build the first React component that displays a list of items.
checklist: Uses useState to hold list data
Renders items with map() and provides a key

steps.title: Create pattern
steps.description: Learn how controlled inputs turn user input into state updates.
steps.contentHtml:
Create with controlled inputs

Controlled inputs keep the field value in React state so the component can validate and submit predictable data.

  • Keep the input value in state via useState.
  • Update state on every onChange event.
  • On submit, append a new item immutably to your list state.
detailedInstructions: Instructor script: Walk through a controlled input example line-by-line: useState hook, value binding, onChange handler, and submit handler.
Tip: Validate input and trim whitespace before adding to the list. Encourage students to test corner cases (empty input, duplicates).

steps.title: Create workflow quiz
steps.description: Review the create pattern and controlled input behavior.

quizQuestions.prompt: What is a controlled input in React?

options.text: An input whose value is stored in state
An input that cannot be edited
An input that only accepts numbers

quizQuestions.explanation: Controlled inputs are driven by React state, so the component controls the input value.

quizQuestions.prompt: Which update is best when adding an item to list state?
quizQuestions.explanation: Using a new array preserves immutable state updates in React.

steps.title: Code exam: add items
steps.description: Implement a form that adds new items to the list.
checklist: Uses useState for the draft input
Adds a new item and clears the input

steps.title: Read pattern
steps.description: Learn how React reads and renders the state data.
steps.contentHtml:
Read data in React

Showing current data in the UI means rendering state values. React keeps the display in sync whenever the state changes.

  • Render arrays with map()
  • Assign keys so React can track each item
  • Use state values directly in JSX

steps.title: Read workflow quiz
steps.description: Confirm that you understand React list rendering.

options.text: Creates rendered JSX list items from data
Sends a request to the server

quizQuestions.explanation: map() transforms each data item into JSX elements for rendering.

quizQuestions.prompt: Why are keys important when rendering lists?

options.text: They help React identify items during renders

quizQuestions.explanation: Keys help React match array items between renders and update only changed nodes.

steps.title: Code exam: add filtering
steps.description: Implement a simple filter so users can search the list.

steps.title: Update pattern
steps.description: Learn how to edit items without mutating state.
steps.contentHtml:
Update items immutably

When updating a specific item, return a new array using map() so React detects the change.

  • Do not mutate the original array.
  • Use prev.map() and replace the updated item.
  • Call setItems(newArray) with the returned array.
detailedInstructions: Instructor notes: Explain why mutating arrays can fail to update the UI — show splice() example versus returning a new array using map().
Live exercise: Ask learners to change one item in the list and observe the UI behavior when using mutable vs immutable updates.

steps.title: Update workflow quiz
steps.description: Confirm the right way to update list state.

quizQuestions.prompt: Which approach is best to update one item in an array?

options.text: map() and return a new array

quizQuestions.explanation: map() creates a new array with the updated item while preserving immutability.

quizQuestions.prompt: Why avoid mutating the original list in React?

options.text: React may not detect the change

quizQuestions.explanation: React detects updates based on new state references, so mutations can be missed.

steps.title: Code exam: edit items
steps.description: Implement a button that updates one item in the list.
checklist: Uses map() to update the selected item
Calls setItems with a new array

steps.title: Delete pattern
steps.description: Learn how to remove items safely with immutable updates.
steps.contentHtml:
Delete items carefully

Deleting an item should create a new list without the removed item, so React recognizes the state change.

  • Use filter() to exclude the deleted item.
  • Do not mutate the original array.
  • Update state with the returned filtered array.

steps.title: Delete workflow quiz
steps.description: Verify your understanding of delete patterns in React.

quizQuestions.prompt: Which function is best for removing an item from an array immutably?
quizQuestions.explanation: filter() creates a new array without the removed item.

quizQuestions.prompt: Why is a new array reference important after delete?

options.text: React can detect the changed state

quizQuestions.explanation: React compares references and re-renders when the state object changes.

steps.title: Code exam: delete items
steps.description: Implement a delete button to remove items from the list.
checklist: Uses filter() to remove the selected item
Updates state with a new array

steps.title: Real-world recap
steps.description: Tie the course back to business use cases.
steps.contentHtml:
Real-world recap

CRUD patterns are the backbone of many applications from logistics dashboards to admin portals.

By practicing these React patterns, you are ready to build reliable data-driven interfaces.

steps.title: Course closing message
steps.description: Finish the premium course with next steps and encouragement.
steps.contentHtml:
Congratulations

You now have a strong React CRUD foundation for real applications.

Continue building on these skills by adding persistence, routing, and validation to your CRUD workflows.

courses.title: Interview FAQ
courses.description: SOLID principles and design patterns practice for interviews.

steps.title: What is SOLID?
steps.description: Review the SOLID principles, abstract/interface differences, and polymorphism for interview readiness.
checklist: Explain the SOLID principles in plain language
steps.contentHtml:
Sub QuestionAnswerMore ExplanationExampleBenefit
What is SOLIDDesign principles for clean codeReduces complexity and future risksAdd new policy without breaking existing codeMaintainable system
SRP - Single ResponsibilityOne class = one jobAvoid mixed responsibilitiesPremiumCalculator only calculates premiumEasy testing
OCP - Open/ClosedExtend, don't modifyAdd features without changing old codeAdd TravelPolicy without changing core logicSafer changes
LSP - Liskov SubstitutionChild behaves like parentNo broken behaviour when substitutingMotorPolicy works as PolicyReliable code
ISP - Interface Segregation PrincipleSmall focused interfacesDon't force unused methodsIClaimable, IRenewable separatedCleaner design
DIP - Dependency InversionDepend on abstractionNot concrete classesClaimService uses IPaymentServiceFlexible + testable
Explain Abstract vs InterfaceAbstract = partial logic, Interface = contractAbstract has code, interface only rulesPolicy vs IClaimableFlexible design
Explain PolymorphismSame method, different behaviourBehaviour depends on objectCalculateClaim varies by policyReusable logic

steps.title: OOP Fundamentals
steps.description: Review key object-oriented programming concepts with short definitions, explanations, examples, and benefits.
checklist: Explain the core OOP concepts clearly
Describe how objects, classes, and encapsulation relate
steps.contentHtml:
Sub QuestionAnswerMore ExplanationExampleBenefit
What is Inheritance?Parent-child relationship of classesA subclass inherits properties and methods from its superclass for hierarchy and reuseclass Dog extends Animal {}Reduces duplication and supports extension
What is Polymorphism?One interface, many formsSame call can do different things depending on the object typeadd(int, int) / add(float) or Dog overrides sound()Makes code more flexible and reusable
What is an Object?Instance of a classObjects are concrete runtime values created from class blueprintsDog myDog = new Dog();Enables real-world modeling in code
What is a Class?Blueprint for objectsClasses define the properties and behavior that objects can haveclass Animal { int age; }Structures code and organizes behavior
What is Encapsulation?Protecting data by restricting accessHide state behind methods and control how it changesprivate int age; public int getAge()Improves security and prevents misuse
What is Abstraction?Hide implementation, show only essentialsSimplifies complex systems by exposing only required behaviorabstract class Shape { abstract void draw(); }Simplifies interfaces and reduces complexity
What is a Constructor?Initializes an object automaticallyCalled when an object is created to set up its initial statepublic Dog() { this.age = 1; }Ensures objects start with valid data

steps.title: Explain Design Patterns
steps.description: Review common design patterns for interview-ready explanations.
checklist: Summarize when to use these design patterns
steps.contentHtml:
Sub QuestionAnswerMore ExplanationExampleBenefit
SingletonOne instance onlyGlobal shared objectLoggerSaves resources
FactoryCreate objects centrallyNo direct newPolicyFactoryClean code
ObserverNotify changesOne-to-many updateEmail alertsAuto updates

steps.title: Explain ARCHITECTURE & SYSTEM
steps.description: Review architecture and system concepts for interview fluency.
checklist: Summarize scalability and polymorphism in architecture
steps.contentHtml:
Sub QuestionAnswerMore ExplanationExampleBenefit
Hybrid ArchitectureUI + API separationFrontend and backend separatedMVC + REST APIClean structure
Monolith vs MicroservicesOne app vs many servicesServices run independentlyPolicy, Claims, Billing separateScalability
ScalabilityHandle more loadAdd resources when neededClaim system during peak trafficPerformance
Compile vs Runtime PolymorphismOverloading vs OverridingCompile-time vs runtime decisionOverload / override methodsFlexibility
Singleton vs FactoryOne instance vs object creationSingleton reused, Factory creates objectsLogger vs PolicyFactoryControl objects

courses.title: Interview FAQ
courses.description: Angular concept interview questions and answers.

chapters.title: Angular Concepts
chapters.description: Core Angular concept explanations and examples

steps.title: AuthGuard & Route Protection
steps.description: Review Angular AuthGuard implementation, lifecycle hooks, NgRx patterns, and how Angular communicates with .NET Core APIs.
checklist: Explain AuthGuard and route access control
Describe all lifecycle hooks in Angular
Understand two-way data binding and routing
steps.contentHtml:
Sub QuestionAnswerMore ExplanationExampleBenefit
What is AuthGuard?A service that controls route access based on authenticationImplements CanActivate interface; returns boolean or Observable<boolean>Redirect unauthenticated users to login pageSecure routes from unauthorized access
How do you implement AuthGuard?Create service implementing CanActivate interfaceCheck authentication status in canActivate() methodAuthGuard checks JWT token validity before navigationControl route access declaratively
What is ngOnChanges?Lifecycle hook called when input properties changeReceives SimpleChanges object with previous/current valuesDetect when @Input() value updates and respondReact to external property changes
What is ngOnInit?Lifecycle hook called once after component initializationBest place for initialization logic and data fetchingLoad user profile data from API on component startInitialize component safely after inputs are bound
What is ngDoCheck?Lifecycle hook called during every change detection runImplement custom change detection logic hereDetect value changes that Angular's default detection missesImplement custom change detection strategies
What is ngAfterContentInit?Lifecycle hook called after ng-content is projectedInitialize logic that depends on projected contentAccess @ContentChild() after content is projectedInteract with content from parent component
What is ngAfterContentChecked?Lifecycle hook called after every check of ng-contentRuns after ngAfterContentInit and after ngDoCheckPerform additional checks after content initializationRespond to content projection changes
What is ngAfterViewInit?Lifecycle hook called after component view is initializedGood place to access DOM elements and child componentsInitialize jQuery plugins or access native DOMWork with view elements safely
What is ngAfterViewChecked?Lifecycle hook called after every check of component viewRuns after ngAfterViewInit and after change detectionPerform additional checks after view renderingUpdate UI elements after view changes
What is ngOnDestroy?Lifecycle hook called before component is destroyedEssential for cleanup and resource releaseUnsubscribe from observables, clear timers, detach event listenersPrevent memory leaks and cleanup resources
What is NgRx?Redux-inspired state management library for AngularManages application state predictably with immutable dataUse Store to hold user data, dispatch actions on loginCentralized state, easier debugging and testing
What are NgRx Actions?Plain objects representing events or user interactionsActions trigger reducers to update stateLoginAction, LogoutAction, FetchUserActionClear intent of what happened in the application
What are NgRx Reducers?Pure functions that specify state changes in response to actionsTake current state and action, return new stateReducer handles LoginAction by adding user to statePredictable state transitions, easy to test
What are NgRx Effects?Manage side effects like HTTP requests in NgRxListen for actions, perform async operations, dispatch new actionsEffect listens for FetchUserAction, calls API, dispatches LoadUserSuccessSeparate side effects from state management
What are NgRx Selectors?Functions to extract specific pieces of stateMemoized selectors prevent unnecessary component updatesselectUserName selector extracts user.name from storeEfficient state access and component optimization
How do Angular and .NET communicate?Angular uses HttpClient to send requests to .NET APIsCommunication over HTTP/HTTPS with JSON payloadsAngular calls GET /api/users/123 to .NET backendSeamless frontend-backend integration
What is HttpClient?Angular service for making HTTP requestsProvides methods for GET, POST, PUT, DELETE requeststhis.http.get('/api/users')Simplified HTTP communication with observables
How do you handle errors in HTTP calls?Use error callbacks in subscribe() or catchError operator.NET backend provides HTTP status codes; Angular handles errorscatchError() maps 404 to user-friendly messageGraceful error handling and user feedback
What is two-way data binding?Automatic synchronization between model and view using ngModelChanges in view update model; model changes update view[(ngModel)]="user.name" creates two-way bindingReactive UI without manual DOM updates
What is Angular Routing?Client-side routing allowing navigation without page reloadsRouterModule handles URL changes and component switchingRoute to /dashboard or /settings without server requestSingle Page Application (SPA) experience
What is Dependency Injection (DI)?Components request dependencies rather than creating themInjector provides instances through constructor, property, or methodUserService injected into component constructorLoose coupling, easier testing with mock services

steps.title: C# Exception Handling
steps.description: Implement a method that divides two integers and demonstrates correct exception handling with specific catch blocks.

hints.guide: // guide hints: Define the method signature
hints.code:

steps.title: What-is-computer
steps.contentHtml:
💻

Chapter 01

What is a Computer?

🤔 Let's Learn!

A computer is a machine that follows instructions! It takes information (input), thinks about it (process), and gives you something back (output).

You're using a computer right now to read this! 🎉

🔧 Parts of a Computer

Computers have many parts that work together:

⌨️ Keyboard
🖥️ Monitor
🖱️ Mouse
🎧 Speaker
🌟 Fun Fact!
The first computers were as big as a whole room! Now we have tiny computers in our watches!

steps.title: What-is-programming
steps.contentHtml:
🎮

Chapter 02

What is Programming?

🚀 Let's Discover!

Programming is giving computers instructions to do fun things!

It's like writing a recipe for a robot! 🍳

💬 Languages We Use

Computers speak many languages! In this book, we'll learn JavaScript!

JavaScript makes websites interactive and fun!

🌟 Fun Fact!
JavaScript was made in only 10 days! But it's one of the most popular languages now!

steps.title: Hello-World
steps.contentHtml:
👋

Chapter 03

Hello World

🧩 Build Your Own Program

Tap the word blocks in the order you want them to print, then press Run!

Tap blocks above to build your program...
CONSOLE OUTPUT
Challenge
🎯 Try to make it print: Hello World !

📖 Order Matters!

A program is a list of instructions that run in order, one after another. This order is called a sequence.

If you mix up the order, you get a mixed-up result - even if you use the exact same instructions!

console.log("Hello"); console.log("World"); console.log("!"); // prints, in this exact order: // Hello // World // !
🌟 Did you know?

"Hello, World!" is the very first program that most programmers ever write. It's a tradition! It teaches that a computer runs your instructions in the exact sequence you write them.

steps.title: HelloWorld-Explain
steps.contentHtml:
👋

Hello World - Explained

CONCEPT 1

📜 What is a Sequence?

A sequence is just a fancy word for "the order things happen in." When you make a sandwich, you do steps in order:

1

Get two pieces of bread

2

Add the filling

3

Put the bread together

If you did step 3 before step 1, it wouldn't work! Computers are the same - they follow instructions in the order you write them, from top to bottom.

CONCEPT 2

💻 Same Words, Different Order

Here's something surprising: using the exact same instructions but in a different order gives you a totally different result!

console.log("Hello"); console.log("World");
✅ Correct Order
🔀 Swapped Order
CONCEPT 3

⚙️ How the Computer Reads Your Program

The computer reads your program like a story - one line, then the next line, then the next - and it never skips ahead or peeks at later lines first.

1

Read line 1 and do exactly what it says.

2

Move to line 2 and do exactly what it says.

3

Keep going until there are no more lines left.

This is why programmers say a program "runs from top to bottom" - and why getting the sequence right matters so much!

🌟 Ready to play?

Now that you know why sequence matters, go back to 03-Hello-World.html and try building the blocks in the right order to print "Hello World !"

steps.title: HelloWorld-quiz
steps.contentHtml:
👋

Hello World Quiz

steps.title: Maze-Walker
steps.contentHtml:
🧭

Maze Walker

Chapter 04 · Commands (Move Up/Down/Left/Right)
MOVES
0
STATUS
🧑
Tap the arrows, or use your keyboard's arrow keys!
Command Log
🧑 Send a command to move!

💻 JavaScript Code

function move(dir) { const next = getNextPosition(player, dir); if (!isWall(next)) { player = next; // command works! } // if it's a wall, nothing happens }
🌟 Did you know?

Each arrow button sends one command straight to the computer: "move up," "move down," "move left," or "move right." The computer obeys instantly - unless a wall is in the way!

steps.title: MazeWalker-quiz
steps.contentHtml:
🧭

Maze Walker Quiz

Chapter 04 · Commands (Move Up/Down/Left/Right)

steps.title: MazeWalker-Explain
steps.contentHtml:
🧭

Maze Walker - Explained

Chapter 04 · Commands (Move Up/Down/Left/Right)
CONCEPT 1

🎮 What is a Command?

A command is a single, simple instruction that tells the computer to do one exact thing right now - like "move up!"

Unlike a whole program with many steps, a command usually does just one job, and the computer does it immediately.

CONCEPT 2

📍 Commands Change Coordinates

Every character in a maze has a position made of two numbers: a row and a column. Each command changes one of those numbers:

  • ⬆️ Up → row goes down by 1
  • ⬇️ Down → row goes up by 1
  • ⬅️ Left → column goes down by 1
  • ➡️ Right → column goes up by 1
if (dir === "up") row = row - 1; if (dir === "down") row = row + 1; if (dir === "left") col = col - 1; if (dir === "right") col = col + 1;
row: 2, col: 2
CONCEPT 3

🧱 What About Walls?

Before moving, the computer checks: "is there a wall at the new spot?" If yes, the command is ignored - the character stays put. If no, the move happens.

1

Get the command (up, down, left, or right).

2

Figure out the new row/column that command would move to.

3

Check if that spot is a wall or off the edge of the maze.

4

If it's clear, move there. If not, do nothing.

🌟 Ready to play?

Now you know how commands move a character step by step and why walls can block them. Go try 04-Maze-Walker.html and guide the walker to the flag!

steps.title: Memory-Match.quiz
steps.contentHtml:
🎯

Quiz Time!

Let's see what you learned about Memory Match
Question 1
You flip these two cards:
🍎
🍎
What will the if statement decide?
They match, so it's a match!
They don't match, flip them back
The game restarts
Question 2
What does an if statement let a program do?
Make a decision by comparing two things
Draw the game board
Count how many cards there are
Question 3
When two cards do not match, which part of the code runs?
The if part
The else part
Neither part runs
Score
☆☆☆
0 / 3
Answer all 3 questions above!
🌟 Remember

if checks something and does one thing when it's true. else is the backup plan for when it's false!

steps.title: Brief
steps.contentHtml:
📚

Little Programmer

Learn to code through play - one fun chapter at a time!
🎂 Age 6–10
🎮 10 Mini-Games
💻 No Experience Needed

📖 About This Book

Little Programmer is an interactive, game-based introduction to coding for young learners. Instead of reading about programming, kids play their way through it - tapping, clicking, and building tiny programs to solve fun puzzles and games.

Each chapter pairs one big programming idea (like commands, events, variables, or loops) with a hands-on mini-game, a friendly explainer, and a short quiz to check understanding - building real coding instincts before kids ever type a line of code.

ℹ️ Book Details

AUTHOR
Jimmy Cooper
AGE RANGE
6–10 years
CHAPTERS
10
🧑‍💻
Jimmy Cooper
Author & Creator, Little Programmer

🧩 What You'll Learn

🖥️ Computers & Programs
📜 Sequence
🧭 Commands
🖱️ Events
🎲 Variables
🔀 If / Compare
🔁 Loops
⚙️ Game Physics

🚀 How to Use This Book

1

Open a chapter and read the short, friendly explanation of the idea.

2

Play the mini-game - it's built entirely around that chapter's concept.

3

Dig deeper with the "Explained" page and its interactive demo.

4

Take the short quiz to check understanding before moving on.

🌟 For parents & teachers: no prior coding knowledge is required - either for the child or for you! Every concept is introduced from scratch, in plain language, with lots of visual feedback.

steps.title: Whack-a-Mole
steps.contentHtml:
🔨🐹

Whack-a-Mole

Chapter 05 · Events (Click)
SCORE
0
TIME
20
Game Status
🔨 Click Start!

📖 What is an Event?

An event is something that happens - like a click!

The computer waits quietly until you click, then it runs your code. That's called "listening" for an event.

💻 JavaScript Code

hole.addEventListener("click", () => { if (hole.classList.contains("up")) { score++; } });
🌟 Did you know?

Every time you click a button, tap a screen, or press a key, you are triggering an event. Programmers write code that says "when this event happens, do this!"

steps.title: Whack-a-Mole-Explain
steps.contentHtml:
🔨🐹

How Whack-a-Mole Works

Let's see what happens inside the computer!

💻 Our Program

hole.addEventListener("click", () => { if (hole.classList.contains("up")) { score++; } });
1

🧠 The Computer Waits

The computer doesn't do anything at first. It just quietly waits and listens for something to happen.

⬇️
2

🐹 A Mole Pops Up

Every second or so, a random hole shows its mole. This happens on its own, without you clicking anything.

⬇️
3

👆 You Click!

The moment you click a hole, that's an event - something just happened!

⬇️
4

🔎 The Computer Checks

"Was a mole actually up in that hole?" If yes - point scored! If not, nothing happens.

⬇️
5

🏆 The Score Updates

The screen instantly shows your new score.

Try It

SCORE
0
🔨 Click Start!
🌟 Remember

Click events don't run in order like a list of steps - they run whenever the click happens. That's what makes them different from a program like Maze Walker!

steps.title: Whack-a-Mole.quiz
steps.contentHtml:
🎯

Quiz Time!

Let's see what you learned about Whack-a-Mole
Question 1
🐹
🐹
🐹
🐹
🐹
🐹
🐹
🐹
🐹
Only ONE mole is really "up." What should you click to score a point?
Any hole you like
The hole with the mole that's up
The Start button again
Question 2
What is an "event" in programming?
Something that happens, like a click
A picture on the screen
A type of maze wall
Question 3
When does the click code actually run?
At the very start, before anything else
Every second, automatically
Only when you click
Score
☆☆☆
0 / 3
Answer all 3 questions above!
🌟 Remember

Click events only run when the click actually happens - the computer is always listening, but it waits patiently until you act!

steps.title: Dice-Battle
steps.contentHtml:
🎲⚔️

Dice Battle

Chapter 06 · Variables + Random Numbers
YOU
0
ROUND
1
COMPUTER
0
🧑 You
🎲
VS
💻 Computer
🎲
Battle Log
Roll the dice to begin!

📖 What are Variables & Random Numbers?

A variable is a box that stores information, like a score. It can change as the program runs.

Random numbers let the computer "roll the dice" - a different number every time, just like a real die!

💻 JavaScript Code

let playerScore = 0; let computerScore = 0; let playerRoll = Math.floor(Math.random() * 6) + 1; let computerRoll = Math.floor(Math.random() * 6) + 1; if (playerRoll > computerRoll) playerScore++;
🌟 Did you know?

Math.random() gives the computer a random decimal between 0 and 1. Multiplying and rounding it turns it into a random whole number, like a dice roll from 1 to 6!

steps.title: Dice-Battle-Explain
steps.contentHtml:
🎲⚔️

How Dice Battle Works

Let's see what happens inside the computer!

💻 Our Program

let playerScore = 0; let playerRoll = Math.floor(Math.random() * 6) + 1; let computerRoll = Math.floor(Math.random() * 6) + 1; if (playerRoll > computerRoll) playerScore++;
1

📦 Variables Are Created

playerScore and computerScore are boxes that start at 0 and will hold a number that can change.

⬇️
2

🎲 The Computer "Rolls"

Math.random() picks a random decimal, and the code turns it into a whole number from 1 to 6 - just like a real die!

⬇️
3

⚖️ The Numbers Are Compared

The program checks: is your roll bigger than the computer's roll?

⬇️
4

➕ A Variable Changes

If you win the round, playerScore goes up by one. The box now holds a new number!

⬇️
5

🔁 It Happens Again

Roll after roll, the variables keep changing until someone wins the battle.

Try It

YOU
0
COMPUTER
0
🎲
VS
🎲
Roll the dice to begin!
🌟 Remember

A variable is like a labeled box - it can hold a number now, and hold a different number later. That's what makes scores, health, and counters possible in every game!

steps.title: Dice-Battle.quiz
steps.contentHtml:
🎯

Quiz Time!

Let's see what you learned about Dice Battle
Question 1
🧑 rolled a 5 · 💻 rolled a 3
⚄
VS
⚂
Who wins the round, and what happens to their score?
You win, so playerScore goes up by 1
Computer wins, so computerScore goes up
Nobody's score changes
Question 2
What is a variable, like playerScore?
A box that stores a value and can change
A picture of a dice
A number that never changes
Question 3
What does Math.random() help the computer do?
Draw a picture
Pick a random number, like a dice roll
Save the game
Score
☆☆☆
0 / 3
Answer all 3 questions above!
🌟 Remember

Variables hold changing information, like a score. Random numbers let the computer make surprising choices, like a dice roll!

steps.title: Memory-Match
steps.contentHtml:
🧠🃏

Memory Match

Chapter 07 · If / Compare
MOVES
0
MATCHES
0 / 8
Game Status
Find all the matching pairs!

📖 What is If / Compare?

An if statement lets the computer make a decision. It compares two things and asks: "Are these the same?"

If the answer is yes, the computer does one thing. If not, it does something else!

💻 JavaScript Code

if (firstCard.symbol === secondCard.symbol) { // It's a match! matches++; } else { // Not a match, flip back }
🌟 Did you know?

The symbol === means "is exactly equal to." Programmers use it inside if statements to compare two values before deciding what to do next.

steps.title: Memory-Match-Explain
steps.contentHtml:
🧠🃏

How Memory Match Works

Let's see what happens inside the computer!

💻 Our Program

if (firstCard.symbol === secondCard.symbol) { // It's a match! matches++; } else { // Not a match, flip back }
1

👆 You Flip Two Cards

You click one card, then another. The computer remembers both symbols.

⬇️
2

🔎 The Computer Compares

It asks an if question: "Is the first symbol the same as the second symbol?"

⬇️
3

✅ If They Match...

The cards stay face up and your match count goes up!

⬇️
4

❌ If They Don't Match...

The else part runs instead - the cards flip back face down after a moment.

⬇️
5

🔁 You Try Again

This if/else check happens every single time you flip two cards, until you find them all!

Try It

MOVES
0
MATCHES
0 / 6
Find all the matching pairs!
🌟 Remember

if and else let a program choose between two paths. The computer always follows whichever path matches what's really true!

steps.title: Simon-Says
steps.contentHtml:
🟢🔴🟡🔵

Simon Says

Chapter 08 · Loop / Repeat
ROUND
0
BEST
0
START
Game Status
Press Start to begin!

📖 What is a Loop?

A loop tells the computer to repeat something, again and again, without writing the same code over and over.

Simon plays back the whole sequence using a loop - one color at a time - and it gets longer every round!

💻 JavaScript Code

for (let i = 0; i < sequence.length; i++) { lightUp(sequence[i]); await wait(500); }
🌟 Did you know?

Loops are everywhere in code - playing a song list, animating a character, or checking every item in a list all use loops so programmers don't have to repeat themselves.

steps.title: Simon-Says-Explain
steps.contentHtml:
🟢🔴🟡🔵

How Simon Says Works

Let's see what happens inside the computer!

💻 Our Program

for (let i = 0; i < sequence.length; i++) { lightUp(sequence[i]); await wait(500); }
1

➕ A New Color Joins

Each round, the computer picks a random color and adds it to the end of the sequence list.

⬇️
2

🔁 The Loop Starts

A for loop goes through the sequence list one color at a time, from the very first to the very last.

⬇️
3

💡 Each Color Lights Up

On every loop step, the computer lights up one pad, waits a moment, then moves to the next.

⬇️
4

🎮 You Repeat It

Now it's your turn to click the same colors, in the same order, using your memory.

⬇️
5

🔂 The Loop Gets Longer

Every round adds one more color, so the loop repeats one extra time. The game keeps growing!

Try It

ROUND
0
START
Press Start to begin!
🌟 Remember

A loop repeats the same steps without needing new code for every color. That's what lets Simon play a sequence of any length!

steps.title: Simon-Says.quiz
steps.contentHtml:
🎯

Quiz Time!

Let's see what you learned about Simon Says
Question 1
🟢 🔴 🟡
Simon just showed this sequence. What do you do next?
Click green, then red, then yellow - in order
Click any 3 colors you like
Click yellow, red, green - backwards
Question 2
What does a loop let a program do?
Repeat the same steps without rewriting them
Store a single number
Compare two cards
Question 3
Why does the sequence take longer to watch each round?
The computer plays slower every round
The loop repeats one more time - a new color was added
It doesn't - it's always the same length
Score
☆☆☆
0 / 3
Answer all 3 questions above!
🌟 Remember

A loop runs the same block of code again and again - each extra round just means the loop repeats one more time!

steps.title: Falling-Catcher-Explain
steps.contentHtml:
🧺🍎

Falling Catcher - Explained

Chapter 10 · Collision Detection + Bounce + Game Physics
CONCEPT 1

📦 Collision Detection

Every game object is really just an invisible rectangle (a "box"). To check if two things are touching, we compare their boxes: their left/right edges and top/bottom edges.

If all four of these are true, the boxes overlap:

  • Box A's left edge is before Box B's right edge
  • Box A's right edge is after Box B's left edge
  • Box A's top edge is before Box B's bottom edge
  • Box A's bottom edge is after Box B's top edge
function isColliding(a, b) { return a.x < b.x + b.w && a.x + a.w > b.x && a.y < b.y + b.h && a.y + a.h > b.y; }
CONCEPT 2

🏓 Bounce

A "bounce" is just a direction flip. Every moving object has a velocity - a speed and direction, often split into vx (left/right speed) and vy (up/down speed).

When the object hits a wall, we multiply that velocity by -1. Same speed, opposite direction!

if (ball.x <= 0 || ball.x + ball.w >= stageWidth) { ball.vx *= -1; // flip left/right direction }
CONCEPT 3

⚙️ Game Physics Loop

Games don't move things all at once - they update a tiny bit, over and over, many times per second. That's the game loop.

1

Move every object a little bit, based on its velocity.

2

Check for collisions (did anything touch a wall, basket, or another object?).

3

React to what happened (bounce, catch, remove, add score).

4

Draw everything in its new position, then repeat - forever, ~60 times a second!

function gameLoop() { moveEverything(); checkCollisions(); requestAnimationFrame(gameLoop); // do it again next frame }
🌟 Ready to play?

Now that you know how collision detection and bounce work, go try 10-Falling-Catcher.html and watch for the moment fruit bounces off the walls or lands in your basket!

steps.title: Falling-Catcher.quiz
steps.contentHtml:
🧺🍎

Falling Catcher Quiz

Chapter 10 · Collision Detection + Bounce + Game Physics

steps.title: Falling-Catcher
steps.contentHtml:
🧺🍎

Falling Catcher

Chapter 10 · Collision Detection + Bounce + Game Physics
SCORE
0
LIVES
3
🧺
⬅️ ➡️ arrow keys, or drag with your finger/mouse
Game Status
🍎 Click Start!

💻 JavaScript Code

// Collision detection (do the boxes overlap?) if (item.x < basket.x + basket.w && item.x + item.w > basket.x && item.y + item.h > basket.y) { score++; } // Bounce off the walls if (item.x <= 0 || item.x + item.w >= stageW) { item.vx *= -1; }
🌟 Did you know?

Collision detection is how a game checks if two things are touching. We compare their invisible boxes! And bounce physics just means: when something hits a wall, flip its direction (multiply speed by -1).

steps.title: Breakout-Explain
steps.contentHtml: Page content

steps.title: Breakout
steps.contentHtml:
🧱🏓

Breakout

Chapter 10 · Collision Detection + Bounce + Game Physics
SCORE
0
LIVES
3
⬅️ ➡️ arrow keys, or drag with your finger/mouse
Game Status
🧱 Click Start!

💻 JavaScript Code

// Ball vs. brick collision if (ball.x < brick.x + brick.w && ball.x + ball.size > brick.x && ball.y < brick.y + brick.h && ball.y + ball.size > brick.y) { ball.vy *= -1; // bounce brick.alive = false; score += 10; }
🌟 Did you know?

Breakout (1976) is one of the first games to use real game physics - a bouncing ball with velocity that reflects off walls, a paddle, and bricks using collision detection!

courses.title: Interview FAQ
courses.description: SQL concept and coding interview practice (code exams).

chapters.title: SQL Concepts & Code Exams
chapters.description: Common SQL interview tasks implemented as code exams.

steps.title: Find Duplicate Records
steps.description: Write a SQL query to identify duplicate rows in a table based on one or more columns.
steps.starterCode:
-- 1: SELECT the columns to check for duplicates
-- 2: GROUP BY those columns
-- 3: HAVING COUNT(*) > 1
-- 4: Return the duplicate values

hints.guide: -- hint1: SELECT the columns to check for duplicates
-- hint2: GROUP BY those columns
-- hint3: HAVING COUNT(*) > 1
-- hint4: Return the duplicate values
hints.code:
SELECT column1, column2, COUNT(*)
FROM YourTable
GROUP BY column1, column2
HAVING COUNT(*) > 1;

steps.title: Products table SQL (CRUD)
steps.description: Practice basic CRUD SQL on a products table.

steps.title: Extract First Name
steps.description: Write a SQL query to fetch only the first name from the FullName column.
steps.starterCode:
-- 1: Select the first name from FullName
-- 2: Use LEFT and CHARINDEX
-- 3: Return FirstName

hints.guide: -- hint1: Select the first name from FullName
hints.code: SELECT LEFT(FullName, CHARINDEX(' ', FullName + ' ') - 1) AS FirstName

hints.guide: -- hint2: Read from EmployeeDetails table

steps.title: Employees with Salary Records
steps.description: Return employee records only when they have a salary record.
steps.starterCode:
-- 1: Select employee fields from EmployeeDetails
-- 2: Inner join EmployeeSalary on EmpId
-- 3: Return matched employee records

hints.guide: -- hint1: Select employee columns from EmployeeDetails
-- hint2: Use INNER JOIN with EmployeeSalary
hints.code:
FROM EmployeeDetails ED
INNER JOIN EmployeeSalary ES ON ED.EmpId = ES.EmpId;

steps.title: Employee Details with Optional Salary
steps.description: Return employee names and salary including employees without salary records.
steps.starterCode:
-- 1: Select employee name and salary
-- 2: Use LEFT JOIN on EmpId
-- 3: Return all employee records

hints.guide: -- hint1: Select employee name and salary
-- hint2: Use LEFT JOIN so employees without salary are included
hints.code:
FROM EmployeeDetails ED
LEFT JOIN EmployeeSalary ES ON ED.EmpId = ES.EmpId;

steps.title: Employees Who Are Managers
steps.description: Fetch all employees who are also managers.
steps.starterCode:
-- 1: Select all columns from EmployeeDetails
-- 2: Filter employees whose EmpId appears as a ManagerId
-- 3: Return manager employee rows

hints.guide: -- hint1: Select all employee columns
-- hint2: Use a subquery to find manager IDs
hints.code:
FROM EmployeeDetails
WHERE EmpId IN (SELECT ManagerId FROM EmployeeDetails WHERE ManagerId IS NOT NULL);

steps.title: Min and Max Salary in One Query
steps.description: Display the employee with minimum and maximum salary in a single query.
steps.starterCode:
-- 1: Use min and max salary in a single query
-- 2: Select employee name, position, salary
-- 3: Combine the two results

hints.guide: -- hint1: Select employee information for minimum salary
hints.code:
SELECT Name, Position, Salary
FROM employee
WHERE Salary = (SELECT MIN(Salary) FROM employee)

hints.guide: -- hint2: Select employee information for maximum salary
hints.code:
UNION
SELECT Name, Position, Salary
FROM employee
WHERE Salary = (SELECT MAX(Salary) FROM employee);

steps.title: Count Employees in Project P1
steps.description: Return the number of employees working in project 'P1'.
steps.starterCode:
-- 1: Use SELECT COUNT(*) to count rows
-- 2: FROM EmployeeDetails
-- 3: WHERE ProjectName = 'P1'
-- 4: Add AS EmployeeCount to label result

hints.guide: -- hint1: Use SELECT COUNT(*) to count rows
-- hint2: FROM EmployeeDetails
-- hint3: WHERE ProjectName = 'P1'
-- hint4: Add AS EmployeeCount to label result

steps.title: Project-wise Employee Count
steps.description: Fetch project-wise employee counts sorted by count descending.
steps.starterCode:
-- 1: Count rows per project in EmployeeSalary
-- 2: Group by project
-- 3: Order results by descending count

hints.guide: -- hint1: Select project and count of EmpId
-- hint2: Read from EmployeeSalary table
-- hint3: Group by project name
-- hint4: Order counts in descending order

steps.title: Remove Duplicate Records
steps.description: Remove duplicate rows from a table without using a temporary table.
steps.starterCode:
-- 1: Use a CTE with ROW_NUMBER() to identify duplicates
-- 2: Delete rows with row number > 1
-- 3: Keep one unique row per group

hints.guide: -- hint1: Define a CTE to rank duplicate rows
hints.code:
WITH RankedData AS (
    SELECT EmpId, FullName, ManagerId, DateOfJoining,
           ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY FullName, ManagerId, DateOfJoining ORDER BY EmpId) AS RowNo
    FROM EmployeeDetails
)

hints.guide: -- hint2: Delete rows where row number is greater than 1

steps.title: Salary Range Query
steps.description: Fetch employee names whose salary is between 5000 and 10000 inclusive.
steps.starterCode:
-- 1: Join EmployeeDetails with EmployeeSalary
-- 2: Filter salary between 5000 and 10000
-- 3: Select employee names

hints.guide: -- hint1: Select employee full names from EmployeeDetails
-- hint2: Join EmployeeSalary with EmployeeDetails by EmpId
hints.code:
FROM EmployeeDetails ED
JOIN EmployeeSalary ES ON ED.EmpId = ES.EmpId

hints.guide: -- hint3: Filter salary between 5000 and 10000
-- hint4: Return employee names for matching records
hints.code:
SELECT ED.FullName
FROM EmployeeDetails ED
JOIN EmployeeSalary ES ON ED.EmpId = ES.EmpId
WHERE ES.Salary >= 5000 AND ES.Salary <= 10000;

steps.title: Second Largest Salary
steps.description: Fetch the second highest salary using ranking or OFFSET.
steps.starterCode:
-- 1: Use ranking or OFFSET to locate the second largest salary
-- 2: Return the salary value or row
-- 3: Keep the query in a single statement

hints.guide: -- hint1: Rank salaries in descending order
hints.code:
WITH RankedSalaries AS (
    SELECT EmployeeID, Salary, RANK() OVER (ORDER BY Salary DESC) AS SalaryRank
    FROM Employees
)

hints.guide: -- hint2: Select the row with rank 2
